title: Compose complex guards for a validation dispatch
prompt: Write lib/my_app/validator.ex (module MyApp.Validator). Implement validate/2 that takes a value and a type spec atom (:positive_int, :non_empty_string, :email_shape, :percentage, :currency_code) and returns :ok | {:error, reason}. Use ONE function-head clause per type spec, each with a `when` guard that checks the value's shape (is_integer, is_binary, byte_size, string pattern). Compose guards with `and`/`or`. The :email_shape guard must require is_binary AND byte_size > 3 AND uses a function-head-level check — but since `String.contains?` is NOT allowed in guards, you must do the '@' check via a binary pattern in a SEPARATE guard-clause function: defp has_at?(s) when is_binary(s). Then the email_shape head calls: def validate(v, :email_shape) when is_binary(v) and byte_size(v) > 3, do: (if has_at?(v), do: :ok, else: {:error, :malformed_email}). Do NOT use cond or case inside validate/2. Fallthrough head: def validate(_, type), do: {:error, {:unknown_type, type}}.
tier_rationale: Requires knowing the exact set of functions allowed in guards (is_*, byte_size, +, -, ==, etc.) and working around the 'String.contains? not allowed' constraint by threading into a separate helper. Sonnet typically tries to call String.match?/Regex.match? from inside a guard and fails.
